speaker: interviewer
text: Amy [? Margolis ?] [? Cohen. ?] This is tape number five. To conclude, can you tell me what your most frightened moment was before you got to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that what always stands out in my mind is being a little girl four, five, six years old, and having this feeling of an ominous thing closing in on us. And it was mostly because I heard adults talking. And I couldn't really figure out what it meant when they said, this is bad for the Jews, and we better be careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. Was some big, terrible monster going to devour us? What was going to happen to us? It seemed so sure that something terrible was going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just growing up with-- with that feeling, I think, affected me in some way, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think it affected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think children that grow up with fear all around them, with fearful parents-- I knew-- I knew my parents love me, but I knew that they were insecure. And that made me feel insecure. Even though we were together, and we got out together, and all of that, there was that general insecurity that Jews weren't allowed here and Jews weren't allowed that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we were Jews. We weren't allowed. We were bad. We-- there was something wrong with us. Because other people had these. They could do other things. We couldn't. There was so much we couldn't do. We were different. What was wrong with us? So you believed some of the propaganda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure, I believed the propaganda. And I think I believed-- probably believed most of it. And I was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, was I physically abused? Was I physically hurt? No way. No way. I think-- there-- but there was that-- that feeling of inadequacy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've had a tough time overcoming that-- How did you overcome--

speaker: interviewee
text: --even in my-- my adult life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you overcome it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had to prove that I could do things. I proved it when I went to school and I got very good grades. I proved it when I became a designer at age 18, when I graduated from high school. That was incredible. I was so young, but I was doing things. I was capable. I was successful. I proved it when I got married and married an American when I was 21 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name was Melvin-- Melvin Jaffe from Pittsburgh. I met him right after he was discharged from the army in-- after the Second World War. And we got married. I was always proving that, you know, I'm very capable, and I can do everything that all the Americans can do. And I had two children like everybody else. And I acquired stuff. Sometimes I smile at it now, but I had to have the fur coat, and I had to have this, and I had to have that. The-- the-- good china, the silver-- it was all very important to me. I think one of the reasons was because I was always trying to-- to prove that I'm OK. I'm OK. And I never thought of it much this way before, actually, but all of this retrospection has made me think of-- think of that. Later on, fortunately, I was able to build a career in education. I always really wanted to go to college. Getting a job at FIT was a wonderful excuse. I had to go to college. It was not something that I did for myself, actually. And I had trouble doing things just for myself for many years. It was OK to do things for the family. It was OK to do things because I had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: But to actually go to school because I wanted to go to school? That wasn't quite allowable. It was allowable to go to school because I had to in order to keep my job. So I need that excuse. It was a wonderful excuse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I was very happy getting-- eventually getting my degree, and going on to write books, and have a good, successful career. Did all kinds of fun things at FIT. It was good to be in administration. That was a wonderful experience for me, and I'm very happy that things turned out the way they did. Unfortunately, my husband had a heart attack when he was quite young. He was only 39 years old then. After that, he lived with a heart condition for another 23 years, thank goodness. And he lived to see his children married. And he knew three of his grandchildren. The littlest one was born after he died. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] now I am alone. I live by myself, but I have a very good friend, Lou Cohen, who is also a widower. And the two of us kind of take care of each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't bring him in right now, since--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you mentioned him. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I-- I would like to introduce Lou Cohen. He's my very good friend, and he has made the last few years of my life very pleasant. And even though I'm alone, I'm no longer-- my husband passed away, but I am really not alone because Lou is there. And I can call on him for, oh, just about everything, anything. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you this about the propaganda and how it affected you long term. Do you still believe any of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the last few years I've gotten over a lot of that-- the insecurity, the always trying to have to prove myself wherever I was. I think Lou's had a lot to do with that. Actually, Lou is a trained psychologist. [LAUGHS] And he has given me a lot of confidence inside. And it-- it-- it has made a difference-- a real difference in my attitude about many things. I'm not as serious as I used to be. I can relax more. I'm not as intense, and that's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: The final question I'm going to ask you is, do you think your experiences as a little girl in Germany and then coming here affected the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very difficult one to answer. I raised my children-- the way they grew up-- I went to-- I went to work. I worked all the time. I was a career woman before it was fashionable. And my emphasis was always on making them independent, taking care of themselves. I encouraged them to do things that they want to do. I don't know whether that was because of my background in Germany, my insecurity. Maybe I wanted them to be more secure. I really don't know. My husband certainly had influence on how children were raised. And, so, this is a very difficult question to answer. Fortunately, they both turned out just fine. And they are both married and have children of their own. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they are a source of great joy to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know Mrs. Jaffe's stories before today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think so. Yeah. We've spoken about it. She told me about it, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there anything else you want to say before we go to the pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. This has been a very interesting experience. I certainly remembered stuff that I thought I had forgotten. And here we are. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well we really want to thank you very much it's very valuable information you're sharing with us. And now just get the last bit of it from the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a picture of me and my sister, taken in 1928, when I was a year old. I'm on the left and the name is Hilde Wolf Jaffe-- H-I-L-D-E W-O-L-F J-A-F-F-E. On the right is my sister Herta-- H-E-R-T-A, Wolf. And we're just posing very nicely for our portrait for the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this? Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was taken in Cologne. It was a photographer in Cologne who took those pictures. In this picture, the bride is my cousin, Ilse-- Ilse Schubach. Her last name is spelled S-C-H-U-B-A-C-H. And her husband's name is Sternshein-- S-T-E-R-N-S-H-E-I-N. I don't remember his first name. Carrying the-- the train is my sister, Herta, and myself, Hilde. The two elderly people standing behind us are Edith's parents, Rosa Schubach-- her maiden name was Wolf, my father's sister-- and her husband Moritz. And they were both deported. And next to them, the tall young man, is Kurt Schubach-- K-U-R-T. And he was able to save himself by going to Sao Paulo, in Brazil. Edith and her husband were shot in Yugoslavia, in-- probably in 1943 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this picture taken and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Beuel, where Edith lived, and it was 1932, I believe. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beuel, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, yes. Beuel is near Bonn. This is our house where I lived from the time I was born until we left Germany. It is in [? Zeurt ?], near Cologne, and the address was Hauptstrasse 66. Hauptstrasse translates into Main Street. On-- the window on the left is the window to my father's butcher store. And the-- the main entrance served both the store and the apartment rooms. On the right is our living room. At the door are my mother, Fanny Wolf, my father, Sally Wolf, who was later Saul Wolf when we came to this country, and my sister Herta-- H-E-R-T-A. And myself, Hilde-- H-I-L-D-E. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It looks like this picture was taken in 1933. This picture was taken on a street in Cologne very shortly before we left Germany. On the left is my father, Sally-- Saul-- Wolf, then my sister, Herta Wolf, and my cousin Fritz-- Fritz Wolf-- who lived with us at the time. Then there is myself, between Fritz and my mother, Hilde, and then my mother, Fanny.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was probably taken, oh, in 1937, just-- as I said-- just before we came here. This is a children's passport. It was my passport, Hilde Wolf. Taken-- the picture was taken in 1937, just before we came here. And on the right, attached on that little white sheet of paper, is the American visa stamp. This was stamped in-- in Stuttgart, where the American consulate was. And it's dated December the sixth. This was the best thing I ever got, even though it has the swastikas all over it. It gave us the right to come to this country. This is a picture of my parents taken soon after we arrived here in 1938. It's taken on the roof of Mount Sinai Hospital, where they had a convalescent division. My mother was recuperating from major surgery. She arrived to this country sick. My father, Saul Wolf and my mother, Fanny Wolf. This is a picture of my cousin Ilse's wedding. The bride is Ilse Salm-- S-A-L-M. And her husband is Hugo Schwarz-- H-U-G-O S-C-H-W-A-R-Z. The picture was taken in 1939. The quotas were closed. None of those people escaped Germany, except for the little boy in the front, which is my cousin [? Mikhail, ?] who is now living in Israel. All the adults, as far as I know, were deported and were killed in the-- in the camps. On the left is my cousin Otto Wolf. Then comes my uncle, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Max Vasen, my aunt, Else Vasen, born Wolf. The next man, I don't know. But Uncle Moritz Salm, the bride's father, and my aunt, Lily Salm, the bride's mother. The next couple I don't know. Then, there is my aunt, Johanna Wolf and my uncle, Max Wolf, who lived in Rheinbach. Incidentally, the picture is taken in Rheinbach. That's where many of the Jews lived before they were deported. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Further on, there is a-- a distant cousin Rudy, or is it Walter? It's-- they were brothers. It's one or the other. And-- No, it's Walter Eis. Rudy was his brother, who was not there because he escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, there is, I think, my aunt, [? Rustian ?] Schubach, and my uncle, Moritz Schubach. The next man I don't know. In the front row, the elderly lady is the bride's grandmother. She was-- her last name was Salm-- S-A-L-M. I don't know her first name-- name, but it's the-- the mother of the father of the bride. And the other elderly lady, I don't know. It must be on the groom's family. And, yes, the little boy looks like it's [? Mikhail, ?] and he's in Israel now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is [? Mikhail's ?] last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Mikhail ?] Maor, M-A-O-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Vasen and Eis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vasen is spelled V-A-S-E-N, and Eis is E-I-S. This is a picture of my grandchildren and my son-in-law. On the left is Jake Rose-- J-A-K-E R-O-S-E. Next to him is Thea Jaffe-- T-H-E-A Jaffe. And then my son-in-law, Craig Rose-- C-R-A-I-G. And my grandson, Sam-- Sam Rose, and then Isaac Jaffe. The picture was taken in August 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Walker Valley, New York, where I have another house. This picture was taken in San Diego, California, in 1992. My daughter and her husband, the Roses, live there. And on the picture are Jake Rose leaning on the chair where his dad, Craig Rose, is sitting. Then there's a picture of my son, Robert Jaffe. Sam Rose is the little boy. And the young woman in the background is my daughter, Barbara-- Barbara Rose. Here is a family picture again, high above Lake Minnewaska, in upstate New York. On the left is myself, and then my daughter-in-law, Anne, my granddaughter, Thea, and my son, Robert Jaffe. This picture was taken in 1994.
